file_name: Rastgoo et al. - 2016 - Tackling the problem of data imbalancing for melanoma classification.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning techniques, specifically comparing data balancing strategies (Over-Sampling, Under-Sampling, NearMiss-2, SMOTE, etc.) combined with a Random Forest (RF) classifier, to perform melanoma classification on dermoscopic images from the PH 2 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the analysis of data balancing strategies (Over-Sampling, Under-Sampling, NearMiss-2, SMOTE, etc.) combined with a Random Forest classifier for melanoma classification. It utilizes the PH 2 dataset, performs feature extraction, conducts cross-validation experiments, and reports new quantitative results (Sensitivity and Specificity metrics) in tables and figures. Although it reviews existing techniques, its primary contribution is the empirical evaluation and comparison of these methods, classifying it as a research paper. It was published in the proceedings of the BIOSTEC 2016 conference.

title: Tackling the Problem of Data Imbalancing for Melanoma Classification
journal: In Proceedings of the 9th International Joint Conference on Biomedical Engineering Systems and Technologies (BIOSTEC 2016) - Volume 2: BIOIMAGING
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of data imbalance in melanoma classification using dermoscopic images from the public PH 2 dataset. The authors evaluated the impact of various data balancing strategies, including Over-Sampling (OS) and Under-Sampling (US) techniques like NearMiss-2 and SMOTE, combined with a Random Forest classifier. Feature extraction utilized color and texture descriptors, specifically comparing performance in both feature and data spaces. The evaluation employed a 10-fold cross-validation scheme to assess Sensitivity and Specificity. The results demonstrated that NearMiss-2 outperformed other methods, achieving a Sensitivity of 91.2% and Specificity of 81.7%. The study concludes that under-sampling and combinations of over- and under-sampling in feature space generally yield superior classification performance.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for classification. The study focuses on the imbalance where melanoma cases are fewer than benign/dysplastic cases. In the experimental subset, there were 39 melanoma lesions.

Grouped with benign lesions as the majority class in the imbalanced dataset. The paper notes that melanoma and dysplastic nevi share similar characteristics. In the experimental subset, these were part of the 117 benign and dysplastic lesions.
Grouped with dysplastic lesions as the majority class. In the experimental subset, these were part of the 117 benign and dysplastic lesions.

all_task_types.details: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into malignant melanoma or benign/dysplastic nevi classes using a Random Forest classifier. The task addresses class imbalance, with performance evaluated via Sensitivity (SE) and Specificity (SP).

datasets_used.name: PH 2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset acquired at the Dermatology Service of Hospital Pedro Hispano, Matosinhos, Portugal, using a Tuentinger Mole Analyzer system with 20x magnification. The images are 8-bit RGB with a resolution of 768x560 pixels. The full dataset contains 200 images with histological ground truth. For this study, a subset was used to address class imbalance.

use.training: Used for training the Random Forest classifier via 10-fold cross-validation on a subset with an imbalance ratio of 1:3 (39 melanoma, 117 benign/dysplastic). Data balancing techniques (OS, US, etc.) were applied to the training folds.
use.validation: Used for validation via stratified 10-fold cross-validation.
use.testing: Used for testing via stratified 10-fold cross-validation.
use.other: The full dataset (200 images) was the source, but only a randomly selected subset of 156 images was used for the experiments to simulate an imbalanced scenario.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To validate the classifier and evaluate classification performance (Sensitivity and Specificity) over 10 runs.
To ensure class distribution is maintained within the folds of the 10-fold cross-validation scheme.
To evaluate the trade-off between Sensitivity (SE) and Specificity (SP) by penalizing incorrect classifications, specifically weighting the cost of missing a melanoma case higher than a benign case.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance comparisons were made using Sensitivity, Specificity, and a Classification Cost function derived from 10-fold cross-validation.

results_obtained_in_each_dataset.extraction_error: Could not parse a balanced JSON object

architecture_details.randomization: Bootstrap sampling at tree level; random subset of features at node level
feature_descriptors: Opponent color angle and hue histogram (C2)

classification_or_output_head.method: Majority vote combination of all trees

preprocessing_techniques: Data space sampling (Over-Sampling and Under-Sampling) applied to original images before feature extraction
Random Deformation using Gaussian Motion (RDGM) for synthetic sample generation
Cubic b-spline interpolation for approximating non-integer points in image deformation
Global rotation (80 degrees) applied during data space transformation
Feature extraction using Color Variance and Histogram (C1), Opponent Color Space Angle and Hue Histogram (C2), Completed Local Binary Pattern (CLBP) (T1), and Gabor Filter (T2)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Randomly selected without replacement a subset of samples from the majority class to equalize class counts.
Generated new minority class samples by interpolating between existing minority samples and their k-nearest neighbors (k=3). Formula: x_j = x_i + σ(x_nn - x_i), where σ is a random number in [0, 1].
Randomly selected without replacement a subset of samples from the majority class to equalize class counts.
Removed pairs of nearest neighbor samples from different classes to clean the dataset boundary.
Applied K-Means clustering to the majority class; centroids of clusters defined the new samples.
Under-sampling method where samples from the majority class are selected such that the average distance to the k nearest neighbors from the minority class is minimum. k=3.
Under-sampling method where samples from the majority class are selected such that the average distance to the k farthest neighbors from the minority class is minimum. k=3.

Under-sampling method where a subset M is generated by finding m nearest neighbors from each minority sample, then selecting samples from M such that the average distance to k nearest neighbors from the minority class is maximum. k=3, m=3.
Under-sampling method applying two rules based on majority vote of k-nearest neighbors. Samples are rejected if their class label differs from the majority vote of their neighbors. k=3.
Combination of SMOTE over-sampling and ENN under-sampling to clean synthetic samples created by SMOTE.
Combination of SMOTE over-sampling and Tomek Links under-sampling to remove overlapping samples created by SMOTE.

Data space augmentation technique for synthetic sample generation. Deformed original images by adding random Gaussian motion N(μ, σ) = (0, 5) at each pixel, compounded with a global rotation of 80 degrees. Used cubic b-spline interpolation for non-integer points.
Data space augmentation technique. Deformed original images using barrel distortion compounded with a global rotation of 145 degrees. Used cubic b-spline interpolation for non-integer points.

conclusions: NearMiss-2 (NM2) outperformed other data balancing strategies, achieving a Sensitivity of 91.2% and Specificity of 81.7% when combined with all feature descriptors (T1,2C1,2).
Methods based on Under-Sampling (US) or combinations of Over-Sampling (OS) and Under-Sampling (US) in feature space generally outperformed other methods.
Balancing techniques are essential for improving classification performance in imbalanced melanoma datasets.
In the feature space, combining color and texture features outperformed using either feature type alone.
Among color features, the Opponent Color Space Angle and Hue Histogram (C2) performed better than Color Variance and Histogram (C1).
Among texture features, the Gabor Filter (T2) outperformed the Completed Local Binary Pattern (CLBP) (T1).
Over-Sampling techniques performed better in data space than in feature space.
Random Under-Sampling (RUS) achieved the second lowest classification cost with a Sensitivity of 92.5% and Specificity of 78.3% using the combined feature set.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single public dataset (PH 2), which restricts the generalizability of the findings to other populations or imaging devices.

The experimental subset used for balancing (39 melanoma, 117 benign/dysplastic) was randomly selected from the full dataset (200 images), potentially introducing selection bias compared to the original class distribution.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to validate the significance of performance differences between balancing techniques.
The Random Forest classifier was configured with 100 trees and un-pruned, which may lead to overfitting given the small sample size of the minority class (39 samples).

The cost function weights (c10=1.5, c01=1) were adopted from previous work (Barata et al., 2013) rather than derived from the specific dataset or clinical utility analysis, which may not reflect optimal clinical trade-offs.

The study focuses on binary classification (melanoma vs. benign/dysplastic) and does not address the clinical challenge of differentiating melanoma from dysplastic nevi specifically, despite noting their similar characteristics.

Data leakage risks are mitigated by applying balancing techniques only within the training folds of the 10-fold cross-validation, but the small subset size (156 images) limits the robustness of the validation.

The results are specific to the PH 2 dataset's acquisition conditions (Tuentinger Mole Analyzer, 20x magnification, 768x560 resolution) and may not transfer to other dermoscopic systems or resolutions.
